---
title: "chromePlus: Chromosome Number Evolution with Binary Traits"
author: "Heath Blackmon"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{chromePlus: Chromosome Number Evolution with Binary Traits}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r setup, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>",
  fig.width = 6,
  fig.height = 4
)
```

## Introduction

chromePlus implements models of chromosome number evolution that extend the
chromEvol framework by incorporating a binary trait that can affect rates of
chromosome gain, loss, polyploidy, and demiploidy. The package is built on the
diversitree framework (FitzJohn 2012), which provides maximum likelihood and
MCMC estimation, handles uncertainty in tip states, and accounts for sampling
incompleteness.

## The chromePlus model

The model supports ten kinds of state transitions. While in binary state 1, a
lineage with $i$ chromosomes may:

- Increase chromosome number to $i+1$ (ascending dysploidy)
- Decrease chromosome number to $i-1$ (descending dysploidy)
- Increase chromosome number to $2i$ (polyploidy)
- Increase chromosome number to $1.5i$ (demi-polyploidy)
- Transition to binary state 2 (with no chromosome number change)

The same transitions are available in binary state 2 with potentially different
rates. For the state-dependent speciation and extinction version (MuSSE), four
additional parameters control speciation and extinction rates in each binary
state.

```{r model-figure, echo=FALSE, out.width="80%"}
knitr::include_graphics("model.png", dpi = 400)
```

**Figure 1:** The chromePlus model showing all possible state transitions.

## Installation

```{r install, eval = FALSE}
# Install from GitHub
install.packages("remotes")
remotes::install_github("coleoguy/chromePlus")
```

## Simulating chromosome evolution

chromePlus provides two simulation functions. `simChrom()` simulates
chromosome evolution on a fixed tree, while `makeSSEchrom()` generates both
a tree and tip data under an SSE model.

```{r simulate-2010}
library(chromePlus)
library(diversitree)

# Create a random tree
set.seed(123)
tree <- rcoal(30)

# Simulate under the simple 2010 chromEvol model
# Parameters: gain, loss, demiploidy, polyploidy, root chromosome number
sim1 <- simChrom(tree = tree,
                 pars = c(0.1, 0.08, 0.0, 0.01, 7),
                 limits = c(3, 15),
                 model = "2010")
head(sim1)
```

```{r simulate-chromplus}
# Simulate under the ChromPlus model with a binary trait.
# Passing state.names removes the asc1/asc2 ambiguity: asc.matched is the
# ascending rate while in the "matched" state, and likewise for "mismatched".
# state.names[1] maps to the first half of the Q-matrix (numeric 0 in the
# unlabeled output); state.names[2] maps to the second half (numeric 1).
sim2 <- simChrom(tree = tree,
  pars = c(asc.matched = 0.1,     asc.mismatched = 0.15,
           desc.matched = 0.08,   desc.mismatched = 0.12,
           dem.matched = 0,       dem.mismatched = 0,
           pol.matched = 0.01,    pol.mismatched = 0.02,
           tran.matched.to.mismatched = 0.05,
           tran.mismatched.to.matched = 0.05,
           root.chrom = 7, root.state = 0),
  limits = c(3, 15),
  model = "ChromPlus",
  state.names = c("matched", "mismatched"))
table(sim2$binary.state)
```

```{r simulate-sse}
# Simulate both tree and data under an SSE model.
# Pass state.names so the returned binary.char is labeled with the state
# names rather than 0/1.
set.seed(42)
sse_result <- makeSSEchrom(
  h = 15, lambda1 = 0.3, lambda2 = 0.3,
  mu1 = 0.1, mu2 = 0.1,
  asc1 = 0.1, asc2 = 0.1,
  desc1 = 0.1, desc2 = 0.1,
  trans1 = 0.05, trans2 = 0.05,
  max.taxa = 30, x0 = 7,
  state.names = c("matched", "mismatched")
)
plot(sse_result$tree, cex = 0.7)
```

## Preparing data for model fitting

The `datatoMatrix()` function converts tip data to the probability matrix
format required by diversitree.

```{r data-prep}
# Create example tip data
# Column 1: species names, Column 2: chromosome number, Column 3: probability
tip_data <- data.frame(
  species = paste0("sp", 1:5),
  chrom = c(5, 6, 7, 8, 10),
  prob = c(1, 1, 1, 1, 1)
)

# Without hyperstate (simple chromEvol model)
mat_simple <- datatoMatrix(x = tip_data, hyper = FALSE)
mat_simple

# With hyperstate (binary trait model)
tip_data_uncertain <- data.frame(
  species = paste0("sp", 1:5),
  chrom = c(5, 6, 7, 8, 10),
  prob = c(0.8, 0.6, 1.0, 0.5, 0.9)
)
mat_hyper <- datatoMatrix(x = tip_data_uncertain, hyper = TRUE)
dim(mat_hyper)
```

## Fitting models

### Simple model: chromosome gain, loss, and polyploidy

The typical workflow uses diversitree's `make.mkn()` to build a likelihood
function, then `constrainMkn()` to apply biologically realistic constraints.

```{r simple-model, eval = FALSE}
# Load your data
tree <- read.tree("my_tree.nwk")
dat <- read.csv("my_tip_data.csv", as.is = TRUE)

# Convert data
dat.mat <- datatoMatrix(x = dat, range = c(18, 80), hyper = FALSE)

# Build and constrain likelihood
lik <- make.mkn(tree = tree, states = dat.mat, k = ncol(dat.mat),
                strict = FALSE, control = list(method = "ode"))

con.lik <- constrainMkn(data = dat.mat, lik = lik, hyper = FALSE,
                         constrain = list(drop.demi = TRUE,
                                          drop.poly = FALSE))
argnames(con.lik)

# Fit by maximum likelihood
n.pars <- length(argnames(con.lik))
fit <- find.mle(con.lik, x.init = startVals(n.pars, min = 0, max = 0.5))
```

### Binary state-dependent chromosome evolution

```{r binary-model, eval = FALSE}
# Data with binary trait probabilities in column 3
dat.mat <- datatoMatrix(x = dat, range = c(18, 80), hyper = TRUE)

lik <- make.mkn(tree = tree, states = dat.mat, k = ncol(dat.mat),
                strict = FALSE, control = list(method = "ode"))

con.lik <- constrainMkn(data = dat.mat, lik = lik, hyper = TRUE,
                          constrain = list(drop.demi = TRUE,
                                           drop.poly = FALSE))
argnames(con.lik)
```

### State-dependent diversification (MuSSE)

To also test whether chromosome evolution is associated with differences in
speciation or extinction rates:

```{r musse-model, eval = FALSE}
dat.mat <- datatoMatrix(x = dat, range = c(18, 80), hyper = TRUE)

lik <- make.musse(tree = tree, states = dat.mat, k = ncol(dat.mat),
                  strict = FALSE, control = list(method = "ode"))

con.lik <- constrainMuSSE(data = dat.mat, lik = lik,
                           s.lambda = FALSE, s.mu = FALSE,
                           hyper = TRUE,
                           constrain = list(drop.demi = TRUE,
                                            drop.poly = FALSE))
argnames(con.lik)

# Fit using MCMC
n.pars <- length(argnames(con.lik))
fit <- find.mle(con.lik, x.init = startVals(n.pars, min = 0, max = 0.5))
samples <- mcmc(con.lik, x.init = coef(fit), w = 1, nsteps = 1000)
```

## Visualizing MCMC results

The `plotChromeplus()` function creates density plots of parameter estimates
from MCMC output with HPD intervals.

```{r plot-example, fig.width=7, fig.height=4}
# Create example MCMC-like data for demonstration
set.seed(42)
mcmc_data <- data.frame(
  gain = rnorm(500, 0.10, 0.02),
  loss = rnorm(500, 0.15, 0.03),
  polyploidy = rnorm(500, 0.02, 0.005)
)

plotChromeplus(
  data = mcmc_data,
  colors = c("#440154", "#21918c", "#fde725"),
  x_title = "rate",
  main_title = "Parameter estimates from MCMC"
)
```

## Constraining models

Both `constrainMkn()` and `constrainMuSSE()` accept constraint arguments to
simplify the full model.

### Main arguments

| Argument     | Effect |
|:-------------|:-------|
| `hyper`      | If `FALSE`, the binary state is ignored |
| `polyploidy` | If `TRUE`, the hyperstate represents ploidy level |
| `oneway`     | If `TRUE`, transitions from state 2 to 1 are set to zero (mkn only) |

### Constraint list options

| Option            | Effect |
|:------------------|:-------|
| `drop.poly`       | Set polyploidy rate to zero |
| `drop.demi`       | Set demiploidy rate to zero |
| `symmetric`       | Force equal rates across binary states |
| `nometa`          | Constrain chromosome rates to be equal across hyperstates |
| `meta`            | `"ARD"` (default) or `"SYM"` for hyperstate transitions |
| `saf.model`       | Use sex chromosome-autosome fusion model (mkn only) |
| `sym.hyperstates` | Equal ascending/descending rates across hyperstates (mkn only) |

### Additional MuSSE arguments

| Argument   | Effect |
|:-----------|:-------|
| `s.lambda` | If `TRUE` (default), a single speciation rate is estimated |
| `s.mu`     | If `TRUE` (default), a single extinction rate is estimated |

## Generating starting values

The `startVals()` helper generates random starting values for optimization:

```{r starting-vals}
# Uniform starting values
startVals(n = 5, min = 0, max = 0.5)

# Normal starting values
startVals(n = 5, mean = 0.1, sd = 0.05, dist = "norm")
```

## References

Blackmon, H., Justison, J., Mayrose, I. and Goldberg, E.E. (2019). Meiotic
drive shapes rates of karyotype evolution in mammals. *Evolution*, 73(3),
511--523.

FitzJohn, R.G. (2012). Diversitree: comparative phylogenetic analyses of
diversification in R. *Methods in Ecology and Evolution*, 3(6), 1084--1092.
